pattern: A bounded-edge shortest path: relaxing all edges exactly k+1 times, each round from the previous round's costs only, finds the cheapest route that uses at most k intermediate stops.
hints: Plain shortest-path ignores the stop limit; you need the cheapest cost reachable using a bounded number of edges.
Run a Bellman-Ford style relaxation for k+1 rounds, and in each round update costs from a frozen snapshot of the previous round so a single round adds at most one more edge to a path.

Keep a distance array seeded with the source at zero; for each of k+1 passes, copy it, relax every flight using the copy as the source of truth, and after the passes return the destination cost or -1 if still unreachable.

edge_inputs.description: cheaper path exists but exceeds the stop budget
one stop allows a two-hop cheaper than the direct flight
zero stops forces the direct flight only
k large enough to use the full chain
two competing routes where the longer hop count is cheaper

stress.description: random directed weighted graphs with a random source, destination and stop budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(2, min(60, max(2, size)))
    edges = {}
    max_edges = n * (n - 1) // 2
    target = rng.randint(0, max_edges)
    attempts = 0
    flights = []
    while len(flights) < target and attempts < target * 4 + 10:
        attempts += 1
        a = rng.randrange(n)
        b = rng.randrange(n)
        if a == b:
            continue
        if (a, b) in edges:
            continue
        edges[(a, b)] = True
        flights.append([a, b, rng.randint(1, 10000)])
    src = rng.randrange(n)
    dst = rng.randrange(n)
    while dst == src:
        dst = rng.randrange(n)
    k = rng.randrange(n)
    return [n, flights, src, dst, k]
stress.note: no parallel edges, src != dst, k in [0, n)
